_title: IEC 60364-5-52:2009+AMD1:2017 — Aluminium Conductor Cable Current Ratings (Annex B)
_basis: IEC 60364-5-52 Annex B — Tables B.52.11 through B.52.14 for aluminium conductors

_units.rating_A: amperes — continuous current at reference conditions
_units.mVAm_2conductor: millivolts per ampere per metre — single-phase (2-conductor loop at operating temperature)
_units.mVAm_3phase_note: For 3-phase balanced circuits: ΔU(phase) = mVAm_2conductor × 0.866 × Ib × L / 1000

_note: Aluminium conductors must not be used below 16mm² due to termination risk and mechanical fragility. Minimum 16mm² throughout. Apply correction factors from part5-52-correction-factors.json. All ratings at reference conditions only.
_termination_warning: Aluminium conductors require tinned copper ferrules or bi-metallic connectors at terminations. Direct aluminium-to-copper contact causes galvanic corrosion. Anti-oxidant jointing compound is mandatory at all stranded aluminium terminations. Check equipment ratings — many devices are rated for copper only.

installation_methods_reference.C: Single-core or multi-core cable clipped direct to a surface
installation_methods_reference.D2: Multi-core cable direct buried in the ground
installation_methods_reference.E: Multi-core cable in free air on a cable tray or ladder
installation_methods_reference.note: Methods A, B, D1 are generally used only with copper conductors for aluminium of sizes used in practice. Large aluminium cables (≥95mm²) are common in underground mains and industrial submains.

method_C_clipped_direct._description: Multi-core aluminium cable clipped directly to a non-thermally insulating wall, ceiling, or cable tray resting against a surface. Most common installation method for aluminium distribution cables in buildings. Sizes below 35mm² rarely used in practice; 50–300mm² is the typical aluminium range.

method_D2_direct_buried._description: Multi-core aluminium cable direct buried in the ground without mechanical protection duct. Standard for LV distribution mains, service cables, and HV cables with armour. Reference depth 0.7m. Apply Cs factor for soil thermal resistivity other than 1.0 K·m/W.

method_E_free_air._description: Multi-core aluminium cable in free air on a perforated cable tray, cable ladder, or cleated to a structure with free air circulation on all sides. Used for large aluminium submain cables routed on cable ladders in risers, plant rooms, or external routes.

aluminium_vs_copper_comparison._note: At the same cross-section, aluminium carries approximately 77–80% of the copper current rating. To replace a copper conductor with aluminium, move up approximately one or two standard sizes.
when_to_use_aluminium: Mains cables and submain cables ≥35mm² where weight and cost are critical
Underground distribution mains (LV network, utility service cables)
Long runs where the smaller cross-section penalty is acceptable against cable cost savings
Aluminium SWA preferred over copper for underground distribution cables in many regions (Nigeria, South Africa, East Africa)
when_not_to_use_aluminium: Final circuits (BS 7671 / IEC 60364-5-52 recommend copper for ≤16mm²)
Flexible cords or cables subject to repeated flexing
Where termination quality control cannot be assured (residential, small domestic)
Circuits where re-termination may be done by unqualified persons

adiabatic_k_values._reference: IEC 60364-4-43, Table 43A — k values for aluminium conductors
adiabatic_k_values._note: Used in fault current check: S ≥ √(I²t) / k. Aluminium has lower k than copper due to lower conductivity and lower melting point.

aluminium_bare_in_air.note: Same as PVC-insulated value — bare aluminium CPC sizing
